era_context: The mid-14th century, a period of significant military and political conflict in Europe and Asia. The Hundred Years' War between England and France was in its early phase. The Yuan Dynasty in China was experiencing internal instability. The Islamic world was politically fragmented. This year is marked by the Battle of Crécy, one of the most famous medieval battles, and the beginning of the end of feudal cavalry dominance in European warfare.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa: virtually no contemporary written records

events.title: Battle of Crécy (August 1346)
events.description: The Battle of Crécy (August 26, 1346) was a major English victory over French forces during the Hundred Years' War. English forces under Edward III, numbering perhaps 10,000–12,000, defeated a much larger French army under Philip VI, estimated at 30,000–40,000. The English victory was achieved through the effective use of longbowmen and disciplined infantry tactics, which devastated the French cavalry charges. The battle marked a significant shift in medieval warfare, demonstrating the decline of feudal cavalry dominance and the rise of missile weapons and infantry tactics. The English victory was a major turning point in the Hundred Years' War and established English military superiority for decades.
key_figures: Edward, the Black Prince (Edward III's son, participant)
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Crécy is one of the most securely dated and well-documented medieval battles. The date (August 26, 1346), location, and general outcome are confirmed by multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty: Toghon Temür restored to power
events.description: In 1346, Toghon Temür (Togto Khan) was restored to power as Yuan Emperor after a period of factional conflict among Mongol nobility. His restoration was engineered by Bayan, a powerful Mongol official. However, Toghon Temür's reign was marked by continued factional strife, corruption, and administrative decay. The restoration did not stabilize the Yuan Dynasty; instead, it marked the beginning of the end of Mongol rule in China. The emperor's reign would be characterized by peasant rebellions, economic decline, and the gradual loss of control over the empire.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official Yuan Dynasty history, compiled by Ming)

events.certainty_note: The restoration of Toghon Temür in 1346 is attested in later official histories, but contemporary sources are sparse. The date and details are less certain than for European events.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English invasion of Normandy
events.description: In 1346, Edward III of England invaded Normandy as part of his campaign in the Hundred Years' War. The invasion was part of a broader English strategy to challenge French control of northern France. The campaign culminated in the Battle of Crécy in August 1346. The English invasion demonstrated Edward III's military ambitions and his willingness to challenge French hegemony in Western Europe.
events.certainty_note: The English invasion of Normandy in 1346 is well-documented in contemporary English and French sources. The campaign and its culmination in the Battle of Crécy are securely dated.

events.title: Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt: Political stability and military strength
events.description: In 1346, the Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt was at a peak of political stability and military strength under Sultan al-Nasir Muhammad. The sultanate controlled Egypt, the Levant, and parts of Arabia. Cairo was one of the world's largest and most prosperous cities. The Mamluk military system, based on slave-soldiers (mamluks), was highly effective. However, the plague would soon begin to impact the sultanate, and this period of stability would not last.

sources.name: Al-Maqrizi, Itti'az al-Hunafa (Egyptian historian, later compilation)
Ibn al-Wardi, Khabar al-Bariyya (Syrian historian, contemporary account)

events.certainty_note: The political situation of the Mamluk Sultanate in 1346 is attested in Islamic sources, but the level of detail varies. The general picture of stability is accepted by modern scholarship.

events.title: Avignon Papacy: Pope Clement VI's reign
events.description: In 1346, Pope Clement VI was reigning at Avignon, the papal seat in southern France. Clement VI (r. 1342–1352) was a powerful and politically active pope who played a significant role in European affairs. He would later respond to the Black Death plague by issuing papal bulls and attempting to protect Jewish communities from persecution. His papacy was marked by political engagement and cultural patronage.
events.certainty_note: The reign of Pope Clement VI is well-documented in papal records and contemporary sources. The date is secure.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Crécy is sometimes portrayed as a decisive turning point in medieval warfare, marking the end of feudal cavalry dominance. However, modern military historians note that cavalry remained important in medieval warfare for decades after Crécy, and that the battle's significance was partly due to French tactical errors and poor leadership rather than English superiority. The longbow is sometimes presented as a revolutionary weapon, but it had been used effectively in earlier English campaigns (e.g., in Scotland). The scale of the English victory is sometimes exaggerated; while the English won decisively, the French army was not entirely destroyed and remained a significant force. The narrative of the Hundred Years' War as a simple conflict between England and France obscures the complex political situation in France, where many French nobles had divided loyalties. The Mamluk Sultanate's stability in 1346 is sometimes overstated; there were factional tensions and administrative challenges even before the plague.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1346 is heavily weighted toward Western European sources, particularly chronicles of the Hundred Years' War. The Battle of Crécy is one of the most thoroughly documented medieval battles, with accounts from English, French, and other sources. Islamic sources are less abundant for 1346 but provide information about the Mamluk Sultanate. Chinese sources are sparse for this year and were compiled after the Yuan's fall. The Hundred Years' War is well-documented from English and French perspectives but less so from the perspective of other regions. Overall, 1346 is a year of moderate to good documentation for Western Europe, with significant gaps for non-European regions. The year is marked by the Battle of Crécy, one of the most famous medieval battles, which has attracted considerable historical attention.

graph_edges.note: English victory at Crécy led to the Siege of Calais in 1347
English invasion of Normandy culminated in the Battle of Crécy
Toghon Temür's restoration in 1346 led to continued instability in 1347
Mamluk stability in 1346 was disrupted by plague in 1347
